response.description_long:
Many teams ship changes only to discover that a bug affects all users. The standard fix — reverting, hotfixing, or deploying at 3 a.m. — is slow and stressful. Shipglass offers an alternative: wrap any change in a feature flag, roll it out incrementally, and roll back instantly if something breaks, without a redeploy.

Shipglass is a feature management platform. Developers use its SDKs (available for 12 languages) to evaluate flags locally from a streamed ruleset, keeping latency under 1 millisecond and working offline. Targeting can combine user attributes, cohorts, and percentage rollouts. Every flag change is audited, and stale flags are surfaced for cleanup. An edge network keeps rulesets within 50 milliseconds of users worldwide.

Any rollout can become an A/B test — attach a metric, and Shipglass computes lift with confidence intervals, eliminating the need for a separate experimentation stack. Common first use cases include risky migrations, tiered entitlements, and gradual mobile rollouts where app-store review makes rollback expensive.

Shipglass was founded in Denver in 2021 by a team of 27 who previously built release tooling at large consumer-software companies. Pricing is free for up to 5 seats, $12 per seat per month for Pro, and Enterprise adds SAML, custom roles, and a dedicated environment. The company explicitly positions against the "homegrown alternative — a config table and a prayer."
